session.title: P/S Core Question Bank
session.disclaimer: AI-generated OpenMCAT practice. Verify explanations when uncertain.

passages.title: Study of Notification Cues and Attention
passages.text: Researchers investigated how smartphone notification cues affect attention and perceived stress. Eighty undergraduate volunteers completed a computerized reaction-time task in three counterbalanced conditions: no notification, a vibration notification, and a visual banner notification. In each condition, participants pressed a key as soon as a target shape appeared. The phone was placed face down for the vibration condition and face up for the visual banner condition. Participants were told that notifications were unrelated to task performance and did not require a response. After each condition, participants rated their momentary stress on a 1 to 7 scale. The researchers also measured whether participants reported checking their phones within 30 seconds after the task.

tables.caption: Mean task performance and stress ratings by notification condition

figureDescriptions.caption: Design summary
figureDescriptions.description: Each participant completed all three notification conditions in randomized order. The primary dependent variables were reaction time, error rate, stress rating, and post-task phone checking.

passages.title: Working Memory, Sleep Restriction, and Retrieval Cues
passages.text: A fictional laboratory study examined how one night of sleep restriction affects memory performance. Participants were randomly assigned to either a normal-sleep group or a restricted-sleep group. The next morning, all participants studied 30 neutral word pairs. After a 20-minute filler task, they completed two tests. In the free-recall test, participants wrote down as many target words as possible. In the cued-recall test, participants were shown the first word of each pair and attempted to retrieve the associated target word. Participants also completed a 2-back task as a measure of working memory updating.

figureDescriptions.caption: Retrieval benefit
figureDescriptions.description: Both groups recalled more words on cued recall than on free recall, but the restricted-sleep group performed worse than the normal-sleep group on both tests.

passages.title: Neighborhood Resources and Stress Physiology
passages.text: A community psychology team studied 120 adults living in three neighborhoods that differed in access to public transportation, grocery stores, and safe recreational spaces. Participants completed a perceived-stress scale, provided a morning saliva sample for cortisol measurement, and reported how often they used neighborhood recreational spaces. The investigators did not randomly assign participants to neighborhoods. They collected age, income, and employment status to use as covariates in later analyses.

figureDescriptions.caption: Conceptual model
figureDescriptions.description: The researchers hypothesized that neighborhood resource access may influence stress partly through daily opportunities for exercise, social interaction, and perceived control.

passages.title: Conditioning and Sound Sensitivity in a Virtual Environment
passages.text: A behavioral science lab used a virtual environment to study how neutral sounds can acquire emotional significance. During acquisition, participants repeatedly heard Tone A immediately before an unpleasant but safe burst of white noise. Tone B was presented equally often but was never followed by the white noise. Skin conductance response was recorded as an index of autonomic arousal. In an extinction phase, both tones were presented without the white noise. In a final test, participants heard Tone C, which was acoustically similar to Tone A but slightly lower in pitch.

tables.caption: Mean skin conductance response in arbitrary units

figureDescriptions.caption: Stimulus arrangement
figureDescriptions.description: Tone A and Tone C were similar in pitch, whereas Tone B was clearly distinct from both.

passages.title: Identity, Group Membership, and Academic Feedback
passages.text: A developmental psychology research group examined how social identity salience affects interpretation of academic feedback. High school students completed a difficult reasoning task and then received standardized written feedback stating that their score was below the sample average. Before receiving feedback, students were randomly assigned to one of two writing prompts. In the personal-values condition, students wrote about an important personal value. In the group-comparison condition, students wrote about ways their school group was viewed by students from a nearby school. After feedback, students rated perceived threat, expected future performance, and willingness to attempt a similar task.

tables.caption: Mean post-feedback ratings on 1 to 7 scales

figureDescriptions.caption: Interpretive note
figureDescriptions.description: All participants received identical task instructions and identical feedback; only the pre-feedback writing prompt differed across conditions.

passages.title: Multisensory Influences on Beverage Perception
passages.text: A behavioral neuroscience lab examined whether ambient odors influence taste judgments. Seventy-two adults sampled the same unsweetened citrus beverage in three counterbalanced conditions: no ambient scent, a congruent lemon scent, and an incongruent coffee scent. Participants were not told that the scent condition changed across trials. After each sample, they rated perceived sweetness, perceived sourness, and purchase intention on 1 to 7 scales. In a separate adaptation task, participants detected faint lemon odors before and after continuous exposure to a moderate lemon scent for 3 minutes.

tables.caption: Mean ratings for identical beverage across ambient scent conditions
Odor detection threshold in arbitrary concentration units

figureDescriptions.caption: Study design
figureDescriptions.description: Each participant experienced all beverage scent conditions in randomized order. The adaptation task was completed after the beverage ratings and used a detection threshold procedure for lemon odor.

passages.title: Stress Appraisal and Working Memory During Social Evaluation
passages.text: Researchers studied how appraisal framing affects stress responses during a brief social-evaluation task. Ninety-six adults were randomly assigned to one of three groups. In the private-reading group, participants read a neutral passage alone. In the evaluative-threat group, participants gave a short speech while being observed and were told that poor performance would indicate weak communication ability. In the evaluative-challenge group, participants gave the same speech while being observed but were told that increased arousal could help them perform effectively. Participants completed a working-memory task afterward and provided saliva samples before and after the task.

figureDescriptions.caption: Appraisal framing
figureDescriptions.description: The evaluative-threat and evaluative-challenge groups completed the same speech task; only the framing statement before the task differed.

passages.title: Adult Education Centers and Health Information Navigation
passages.text: A community research team evaluated a short workshop designed to improve adults' ability to interpret online health information. The workshop taught participants how to identify source credibility, distinguish testimonials from population-level evidence, and compare numerical risks. Participants attended one of three adult education centers located in districts that differed in occupational composition and average household income. Workshop attendance was optional, so participants were not randomly assigned to attend. All participants completed a health-information comprehension test at baseline and four weeks later.

tables.caption: Mean health-information comprehension scores out of 100

figureDescriptions.caption: Resource context
figureDescriptions.description: The researchers proposed that broadband access and predictable work schedules may influence the ability to practice health-information skills outside the workshop.

passages.title: Recycling Behavior and Reinforcement Schedules in Dormitory Kitchens
passages.text: A campus sustainability team tested behavioral strategies for improving recycling accuracy in dormitory kitchens. Twenty dormitory floors were randomly assigned to one of four conditions: informational signs only, a fixed-ratio points program in which floors earned a reward after every fifth correctly sorted item, a variable-ratio points program in which floors earned rewards unpredictably after an average of five correctly sorted items, or an observational-modeling condition in which residents watched a peer demonstrate correct sorting before signs were posted. In the reward conditions, a green light on the sorting station indicated that the sensor was active and points could be earned; a red light indicated that the sensor was off. After four weeks, point rewards were removed for one week while signs remained.

tables.caption: Mean percentage of items correctly sorted

figureDescriptions.caption: Assignment unit
figureDescriptions.description: The dormitory floor, rather than the individual resident, was the unit of assignment because rewards and signs were shared within floor kitchens.

passages.title: Mental Health Messaging, Stigma, and Help-Seeking Intentions
passages.text: A campus research group tested how different messages influence responses to a fictional vignette about a student named R who has experienced repeated episodes of sudden intense fear, rapid heartbeat, trembling, and worry about having another episode. Participants were randomly assigned to read one of four messages before viewing the vignette: a neutral campus-services message, a biological-explanation message, a coping-continuum message emphasizing that distress varies in degree and can be managed with support, or a danger-focused message emphasizing rare extreme outcomes. Participants then rated recognition of clinically significant distress, stigma, and willingness to recommend help-seeking.

figureDescriptions.caption: Ethical note
figureDescriptions.description: The researchers debriefed participants after the study and provided a list of campus and community support resources. The vignette was fictional and was not used to diagnose participants.

questions.stem: A participant can detect a dim light on 50% of trials when it is presented in a dark room. In psychophysics, this light intensity is best described as the participant's:
questions.explanation: An absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus intensity detected 50% of the time under specified conditions. The stem describes detection of a single faint stimulus, not detection of a change between two stimuli or a decision bias.

choiceExplanations.A: A difference threshold is the smallest detectable difference between two stimulus intensities, not the minimum detectable intensity of one stimulus.
choiceExplanations.B: This is correct. Detecting a stimulus on 50% of trials defines the absolute threshold in a typical psychophysical task.
choiceExplanations.C: Sensory adaptation refers to reduced responsiveness after repeated or sustained stimulation, which is not the focus of the stem.
choiceExplanations.D: A signal detection criterion is the decision rule used to say a stimulus is present; it is related but not the threshold described here.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing the absolute threshold for detecting a stimulus with the difference threshold for detecting a change between stimuli.

questions.stem: A student studies Spanish vocabulary in the same quiet library room each day. On test day, the student notices that recalling the words feels easier when sitting in that same room than when sitting in a noisy cafeteria. Which memory principle best explains this observation?
questions.explanation: Context-dependent retrieval occurs when memory is improved because the retrieval context overlaps with the encoding context. The same physical environment can serve as a retrieval cue for information learned there.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The library context present during encoding also aids retrieval.
choiceExplanations.B: Proactive interference occurs when older learning disrupts newer learning; the stem does not describe interference from previously learned material.
choiceExplanations.C: Procedural memory concerns skills and habits, not recall of vocabulary words in a matching environment.
choiceExplanations.D: A source monitoring error involves misattributing where information came from, which is not described.

questions.commonMistake: Treating any improvement in memory as simple rehearsal rather than identifying the role of environmental retrieval cues.

questions.stem: A researcher studying decision-making finds that participants estimate shark attacks as more common than diabetes-related deaths after watching a dramatic documentary about shark attacks. Which cognitive heuristic most directly explains this judgment?
questions.explanation: The availability heuristic occurs when people estimate likelihood based on how easily examples come to mind. The documentary made shark attacks more mentally accessible, biasing frequency judgments.

choiceExplanations.A: The representativeness heuristic involves judging category membership by similarity to a prototype, not by ease of recall.
choiceExplanations.B: This is correct. Recently vivid examples are more available in memory and can inflate perceived frequency.
choiceExplanations.C: Algorithmic reasoning refers to systematic step-by-step problem solving, which would reduce rather than explain this bias.
choiceExplanations.D: A conservation error is associated with cognitive development tasks, not adult probability estimation after vivid exposure.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing availability, which depends on ease of recall, with representativeness, which depends on perceived similarity.

questions.stem: A patient in a fictional educational case has damage to the hippocampus after a laboratory accident. Which outcome would be most expected?

choices.text: Impaired formation of new explicit memories

questions.explanation: The hippocampus is important for forming new explicit, declarative memories. Damage often produces difficulty encoding new facts and events, while many reflexive and sensory functions remain mediated by other structures.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The hippocampus supports consolidation of new explicit memories.
choiceExplanations.B: Spinal reflexes are mediated by spinal circuits and do not depend on the hippocampus.
choiceExplanations.C: Light detection begins with retinal photoreceptors and visual pathways, not the hippocampus.
choiceExplanations.D: Autonomic regulation involves hypothalamic, brainstem, and peripheral mechanisms more directly than the hippocampus.

questions.commonMistake: Overgeneralizing brain damage as causing broad impairment rather than linking the hippocampus specifically to declarative memory formation.

questions.stem: A child receives a small sticker each time she completes her reading homework. After collecting ten stickers, she can trade them for extra playtime. This arrangement is best described as:

choices.text: Classical conditioning using an unconditioned stimulus
Operant conditioning using a token economy

questions.explanation: The stickers function as tokens earned after a desired behavior and later exchanged for a reinforcer. This is an operant conditioning arrangement because consequences are used to increase homework completion.

choiceExplanations.A: Classical conditioning pairs stimuli to elicit reflexive responses; the stem involves consequences following voluntary behavior.
choiceExplanations.B: This is correct. Stickers are tokens that can be exchanged for a backup reinforcer.
choiceExplanations.C: Negative punishment removes a desirable stimulus to reduce behavior, whereas the child gains stickers to increase behavior.
choiceExplanations.D: Latent learning occurs without obvious reinforcement, but the stem explicitly uses reinforcement.

questions.commonMistake: Mislabeling any learned association as classical conditioning even when the behavior is shaped by consequences.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the reaction-time data in Table 1?

choices.text: Vibration notifications were associated with slower responses than the no-notification condition.
Visual banner notifications eliminated attentional distraction.
Notification condition had no relationship to performance because all error rates were below 10%.
Vibration notifications improved target detection by increasing arousal.

questions.explanation: The mean reaction time was 420 ms with no notification and 470 ms with vibration notification. This supports the conclusion that vibration notifications were associated with slower responses in this task. The data do not show elimination of distraction or improved detection.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The vibration condition had the slowest mean reaction time.
choiceExplanations.B: The visual banner condition still had a slower mean reaction time and higher error rate than no notification, so distraction was not eliminated.
choiceExplanations.C: Low error rates do not imply no performance relationship; reaction time varied across conditions.
choiceExplanations.D: The vibration condition was associated with slower responses and higher errors, not improved detection.

questions.commonMistake: Ignoring reaction-time differences because the error rates appear numerically small.

questions.stem: The vibration notification most likely increased phone checking because the vibration had previously been paired with incoming messages in participants' daily lives. In learning terms, the vibration would best be described as a:
questions.explanation: A phone vibration can become a conditioned stimulus if it is repeatedly paired with meaningful notifications. It can then evoke orienting, checking, or arousal even when the current message does not require a response.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. A formerly neutral vibration can acquire meaning through repeated association with messages.
choiceExplanations.B: An unconditioned stimulus naturally elicits a response without prior learning; a phone vibration does not inherently signal social information.
choiceExplanations.C: A negative reinforcer increases behavior by removing an aversive stimulus, which is not what the vibration itself represents.
choiceExplanations.D: A punisher decreases behavior when applied or removed; the observed checking behavior increased after notifications.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing a cue that predicts an event with a reinforcer that follows and strengthens a behavior.

questions.stem: Which feature of the study design most directly helps reduce individual-difference confounding across notification conditions?

choices.text: Each participant completed all three notification conditions.
The phone was face down during the vibration condition.
Participants rated stress after each condition.
Participants were told notifications did not require a response.

questions.explanation: A within-subject design means each participant serves as their own comparison across conditions. This reduces confounding from stable individual differences, such as baseline reaction speed or general phone attachment.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Repeated measurement across all conditions controls many person-level differences.
choiceExplanations.B: Phone orientation defines part of the manipulation but does not control individual differences across conditions as directly.
choiceExplanations.C: Stress ratings provide a dependent variable; they do not by themselves reduce confounding.
choiceExplanations.D: This instruction standardizes expectations but does not control individual differences as strongly as the within-subject design.

questions.commonMistake: Selecting a measurement procedure rather than the design feature that makes participants comparable across conditions.

questions.stem: Which additional result would most strengthen the interpretation that notifications disrupted selective attention rather than only increasing motor slowing?

choices.text: Participants showed greater slowing on trials in which distracting shapes appeared near the target.
Participants had the same resting heart rate before each condition.
Participants reported owning phones for similar numbers of years.
Participants completed the visual banner condition before the vibration condition.

questions.explanation: Selective attention involves prioritizing task-relevant stimuli while filtering distractors. A disproportionate slowing when distracting shapes are present would support an attentional filtering explanation rather than a general motor slowing explanation.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It links the notification effect specifically to distractor processing and attentional control.
choiceExplanations.B: Equivalent resting heart rate would address baseline physiology but not distinguish attentional disruption from motor slowing.
choiceExplanations.C: Similar phone ownership duration would not directly test the mechanism of slower task responses.
choiceExplanations.D: A fixed order could introduce order effects and would not clarify the attentional mechanism.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing a general control variable instead of a result that distinguishes between two competing mechanisms.

questions.stem: Based on Table 2, what is the approximate percentage-point increase in phone checking from the no-notification condition to the vibration condition?
questions.explanation: Phone checking occurred in 12% of the no-notification condition and 48% of the vibration condition. The increase is 48 minus 12, or 36 percentage points.

choiceExplanations.A: This subtracts 12 from 31, comparing visual banner to no notification rather than vibration to no notification.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the visual banner percentage, not the increase from no notification to vibration.
choiceExplanations.C: This is correct. The difference is 48% minus 12% = 36 percentage points.
choiceExplanations.D: This is the vibration percentage itself, not the increase relative to no notification.

questions.commonMistake: Reporting the final percentage rather than the percentage-point difference between conditions.

questions.stem: A worker feels anxious whenever a supervisor walks by because the supervisor often assigns extra tasks without warning. After several weeks in which the supervisor walks by without assigning extra tasks, the worker's anxiety gradually decreases. Which learning process is most directly illustrated by the decrease in anxiety?
questions.explanation: Extinction in classical conditioning occurs when a conditioned stimulus is repeatedly presented without the unconditioned stimulus, reducing the conditioned response. The supervisor walking by no longer predicts extra tasks, so anxiety decreases.

choiceExplanations.A: Stimulus generalization would involve similar cues also producing anxiety, which is not the main process described.
choiceExplanations.B: This is correct. The predictive cue is repeatedly presented without the expected outcome.
choiceExplanations.C: Positive punishment adds an unpleasant consequence to reduce behavior, but the stem describes a conditioned emotional response decreasing.
choiceExplanations.D: Observational learning involves learning by watching others, which is not described.

questions.commonMistake: Mistaking a reduced conditioned emotional response for punishment rather than extinction.

questions.stem: A sociologist argues that schools reproduce inequality by rewarding cultural knowledge and interaction styles that are more common among higher-income families. This argument most closely reflects which concept?
questions.explanation: Cultural capital refers to nonfinancial social assets, such as language styles, tastes, knowledge, and dispositions, that can provide advantages in institutional settings. The scenario focuses on how schools reward class-linked cultural resources.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The argument concerns unequal rewards for class-linked cultural knowledge and interaction styles.
choiceExplanations.B: Role strain involves tension within a single social role, not institutional reproduction of inequality.
choiceExplanations.C: Primary deviance refers to initial norm violation before deviant identity is internalized, which is unrelated to the school reward system described.
choiceExplanations.D: Social facilitation is improved performance in the presence of others on simple or well-learned tasks.

questions.commonMistake: Reducing social class to income alone and missing the role of nonfinancial resources in institutional advantage.

questions.stem: In a fictional survey, adults reporting chronic job insecurity also report frequent headaches and sleep difficulty. Which biological pathway most directly links chronic stress to these physical outcomes?

choices.text: Activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and sustained glucocorticoid release
Inactivation of all sympathetic nervous system responses
Permanent loss of sensory receptor transduction
Immediate elimination of negative feedback in the pancreas

questions.explanation: Chronic stress can activate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, increasing glucocorticoid exposure and affecting sleep, immune function, and other physiological systems. The other choices describe overly broad or unrelated mechanisms.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The HPA axis is a central biological pathway in stress physiology.
choiceExplanations.B: Stress generally activates, rather than inactivates, sympathetic responses.
choiceExplanations.C: Stress does not require permanent loss of sensory transduction to cause headaches or sleep difficulty.
choiceExplanations.D: The pancreas is not the primary pathway linking psychosocial stress to the outcomes described.

questions.commonMistake: Treating stress as purely psychological rather than recognizing neuroendocrine mechanisms.

questions.stem: An adolescent explores several religious, occupational, and political possibilities but has not yet made stable commitments. According to Marcia's identity status framework, this adolescent is best described as being in:
questions.explanation: Identity moratorium involves active exploration without firm commitment. The adolescent is considering alternatives but has not yet settled on stable commitments.

choiceExplanations.A: Identity diffusion involves neither active exploration nor commitment.
choiceExplanations.B: Identity foreclosure involves commitment without sufficient exploration, often adopting others' expectations.
choiceExplanations.C: This is correct. The adolescent is exploring options but has not committed.
choiceExplanations.D: Identity achievement involves commitment after exploration.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing moratorium with diffusion because both involve lack of final commitment, while only moratorium involves active exploration.

questions.stem: A researcher wants to measure whether participants can detect a faint tone while accounting separately for sensory sensitivity and willingness to say, "I heard it." Which framework is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Signal detection theory distinguishes actual sensitivity from response criterion. It is useful when hits, misses, false alarms, and correct rejections are affected by both sensory ability and decision bias.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Signal detection theory separates sensitivity from response bias.
choiceExplanations.B: Social constructionism concerns how meanings and categories are created through social processes, not sensory decision thresholds.
choiceExplanations.C: Drive reduction theory concerns motivation to reduce physiological needs, not detection judgments.
choiceExplanations.D: Cognitive dissonance theory concerns discomfort from inconsistent beliefs or behaviors.

questions.commonMistake: Using absolute threshold alone when the study specifically asks to separate sensitivity from decision criterion.

questions.stem: Which interpretation is best supported by the difference between free recall and cued recall in both groups?

choices.text: Retrieval cues improved access to encoded information.
Cued recall prevented encoding from occurring.
Free recall measured only sensory memory.
The restricted-sleep group had no stored memories of the word pairs.

questions.explanation: Both groups recalled more words when given the first word of each pair. This pattern indicates that retrieval cues can improve access to information that was encoded and stored, even when free recall is lower.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Cues helped participants retrieve more target words.
choiceExplanations.B: Cued recall occurs at test, after encoding; it does not prevent encoding.
choiceExplanations.C: Free recall of studied words after a filler task is not a measure of sensory memory.
choiceExplanations.D: The restricted-sleep group recalled many words, especially with cues, so stored memories were not absent.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming lower free recall means information was never stored, rather than considering retrieval support.

questions.stem: The 2-back task used in the study most directly measures which cognitive process?
questions.explanation: A 2-back task requires participants to monitor incoming stimuli and compare each current item with one presented two steps earlier. This requires working memory maintenance and updating.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The task requires active updating of recently presented information.
choiceExplanations.B: Classical conditioning involves learned associations between stimuli, not tracking recent items in memory.
choiceExplanations.C: An absolute sensory threshold is the minimum detectable stimulus intensity, which the 2-back task does not assess.
choiceExplanations.D: Social identity formation concerns self-concept and group membership, not short-term cognitive updating.

questions.commonMistake: Treating any task involving remembered information as long-term memory rather than working memory.

questions.stem: Which design feature best supports a causal interpretation of the effect of sleep restriction on memory performance?

choices.text: Participants were randomly assigned to normal-sleep or restricted-sleep groups.
Participants completed both free-recall and cued-recall tests.
Participants rated their motivation after the study.

questions.explanation: Random assignment helps distribute potential confounds across groups, strengthening causal inference about sleep restriction. Multiple outcome measures and neutral stimuli may improve measurement, but they do not support causality as directly.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Random assignment is the strongest listed feature for causal inference.
choiceExplanations.B: Using two memory tests provides more information about retrieval but does not by itself establish causality.
choiceExplanations.C: Neutral word pairs may control emotional content but do not eliminate group differences without random assignment.
choiceExplanations.D: Motivation ratings help evaluate a possible alternative explanation but do not create equivalent groups.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing a useful measurement feature rather than the feature that most directly supports causal inference.

questions.stem: Which alternative explanation for the memory findings is most directly weakened by Table 2?

choices.text: The restricted-sleep group was much less motivated than the normal-sleep group.
The restricted-sleep group experienced greater fatigue than the normal-sleep group.
The cued-recall test provided retrieval cues.
The sleep manipulation affected working memory.

questions.explanation: Motivation ratings were similar across groups: 5.8 in the normal-sleep group and 5.6 in the restricted-sleep group. This weakens the claim that lower performance was primarily due to substantially lower motivation in the restricted-sleep group.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The motivation scores are nearly the same, so motivation differences are unlikely to fully explain the performance gap.
choiceExplanations.B: Table 2 supports, rather than weakens, greater fatigue in the restricted-sleep group.
choiceExplanations.C: The existence of retrieval cues is part of the task structure, not an alternative explanation weakened by motivation and fatigue data.
choiceExplanations.D: The 2-back results are consistent with an effect on working memory, not weakened by Table 2.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming any self-report difference is a confound instead of checking the direction and size of the reported values.

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, what is the approximate difference in 2-back accuracy between the normal-sleep and restricted-sleep groups?
questions.explanation: The normal-sleep group had 87% accuracy and the restricted-sleep group had 73% accuracy. The difference is 87 minus 73, or 14 percentage points.

choiceExplanations.A: This is closer to the difference in free recall means than to the 2-back accuracy difference.
choiceExplanations.B: This underestimates the 2-back difference.
choiceExplanations.C: This is correct. The 2-back accuracy difference is 14 percentage points.
choiceExplanations.D: This is larger than the observed accuracy difference.

questions.commonMistake: Mixing the word-recall columns with the percentage-accuracy column.

questions.stem: A child understands that the amount of water remains the same when it is poured from a short wide glass into a tall narrow glass. In Piaget's theory, this understanding is called:
questions.explanation: Conservation is the understanding that quantity remains the same despite changes in shape or appearance. The water task is a classic example of conservation of liquid volume.

choiceExplanations.A: Object permanence is the understanding that objects continue to exist when out of sight.
choiceExplanations.B: This is correct. The child recognizes that volume is conserved across container shape changes.
choiceExplanations.C: Egocentrism is difficulty taking another person's perspective, not understanding volume constancy.
choiceExplanations.D: Formal operational reasoning involves abstract and hypothetical reasoning, which is broader than the conservation judgment described.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing concrete operational conservation with formal operational abstract reasoning.

questions.stem: A fictional client reports persistent low mood, loss of interest in usual activities, disrupted sleep, and feelings of worthlessness for several weeks. For MCAT-level classification, this pattern is most consistent with symptoms of:
questions.explanation: The symptom cluster of sustained low mood, anhedonia, sleep disruption, and worthlessness is characteristic of depressive disorders, especially major depressive disorder in broad MCAT-level classification. This is an educational classification, not a diagnosis.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The pattern aligns most closely with depressive symptoms.
choiceExplanations.B: Specific phobia centers on intense fear of a specific object or situation, not persistent low mood and anhedonia.
choiceExplanations.C: Dissociative identity disorder involves distinct identity states and memory disruption, which are not described.
choiceExplanations.D: Antisocial personality disorder involves pervasive disregard for others' rights and social norms, which is not described.

questions.commonMistake: Focusing on one symptom such as sleep disruption rather than the overall depressive symptom pattern.

questions.stem: A researcher finds that participants from lower-income neighborhoods report higher exposure to environmental noise and higher perceived stress. The researcher concludes that low income directly causes stress. Which critique is most appropriate?

choices.text: The conclusion may be too strong because the observational design cannot rule out confounding variables.
The conclusion is invalid because stress cannot be measured by self-report.
The conclusion is certain because income is a biological variable.
The conclusion is invalid because neighborhood noise is unrelated to stress responses.

questions.explanation: An observational association does not by itself establish direct causation. Other variables, such as housing quality, work demands, discrimination, health status, or safety concerns, could contribute to the relationship.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The causal claim exceeds what the observational design can establish.
choiceExplanations.B: Self-report can be a valid measurement method, though it has limitations.
choiceExplanations.C: Income is socioeconomic rather than biological, and an association alone does not prove causation.
choiceExplanations.D: Noise exposure can plausibly relate to stress, so the issue is causal inference rather than impossibility.

questions.commonMistake: Treating correlation as causation because the proposed causal pathway is plausible.

questions.stem: A student hears a loud crash and immediately experiences increased heart rate and sweating. A few seconds later, the student identifies the sound as a dropped metal tray and begins to calm down. Which nervous system division most directly produced the immediate increase in heart rate and sweating?

choices.text: Sympathetic division of the autonomic nervous system
Parasympathetic division of the autonomic nervous system
Somatic motor division controlling skeletal muscles only
Enteric nervous system controlling digestion only

questions.explanation: The sympathetic division supports fight-or-flight responses, including increased heart rate and sweating. Calming after reappraisal involves reduced sympathetic activation and increased parasympathetic influence.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Sympathetic activation produces the immediate arousal response.
choiceExplanations.B: Parasympathetic activity generally supports rest-and-digest functions and slowing of heart rate.
choiceExplanations.C: The somatic motor division controls voluntary skeletal muscle movement, not sweating and heart rate directly.
choiceExplanations.D: The enteric nervous system regulates gastrointestinal functions and is not the direct source of the described arousal response.

questions.commonMistake: Attributing all rapid bodily responses to voluntary somatic control rather than autonomic sympathetic activation.

questions.stem: A sociologist studies brief face-to-face interactions between bus drivers and passengers to understand how politeness rituals maintain a sense of social order. This focus is most characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Microsociology focuses on small-scale social interactions and meanings in everyday life. Studying politeness rituals in brief interpersonal encounters fits this level of analysis.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The study examines small-scale interactions.
choiceExplanations.B: World-systems theory examines global economic relationships among countries or regions.
choiceExplanations.C: Demographic transition theory concerns population changes associated with industrialization and development.
choiceExplanations.D: Structural mobility concerns movement caused by changes in the social hierarchy, not microlevel interaction rituals.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming all sociology focuses on large institutions and missing the microlevel interaction focus.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is most justified by the data in Table 1?

choices.text: Lower neighborhood resource level is associated with higher perceived stress and higher morning cortisol.
Neighborhood resource level causes cortisol changes independently of income.
Recreational-space use has no relationship to stress.
Income is unrelated to neighborhood resource level.

questions.explanation: The table shows that as neighborhood resource level decreases from high to low, perceived stress rises from 13.1 to 22.0 and morning cortisol rises from 11.2 to 16.7 nmol/L. Because participants were not randomly assigned to neighborhoods, a causal conclusion is not warranted from these data alone.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The table supports an association between resource level and both stress indicators.
choiceExplanations.B: The observational design and income differences prevent a strong independent causal conclusion.
choiceExplanations.C: Recreational-space use varies with resource level in a direction consistent with stress differences; the table does not show no relationship.
choiceExplanations.D: Mean income differs across resource levels, so this statement is contradicted by the table.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting an observational gradient as proof of independent causation.

questions.stem: The investigators included income and employment status as covariates primarily because these variables could:

choices.text: Act as confounders related to both neighborhood resources and stress outcomes.
Guarantee that the study becomes a randomized experiment.
Eliminate the need for measuring cortisol.
Convert perceived stress into an independent variable.

questions.explanation: Income and employment status may influence where people live and may also influence stress physiology and perceived stress. Including them as covariates helps evaluate whether the neighborhood association remains after accounting for these potential confounds.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Covariates can help address plausible confounding in observational research.
choiceExplanations.B: Statistical adjustment does not make a nonrandomized study equivalent to a randomized experiment.
choiceExplanations.C: Cortisol remains a separate physiological outcome measure even if covariates are included.
choiceExplanations.D: Perceived stress remains an outcome measure in the described analysis.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming covariate adjustment fully solves the limitations of nonrandomized research.

questions.stem: Which sociological perspective would most likely emphasize that unequal neighborhood resources reflect competition over scarce material advantages and contribute to health inequality?
questions.explanation: Conflict theory emphasizes power, inequality, and competition over resources. The passage concerns unequal neighborhood resources and stress-related health indicators, making conflict theory the best match.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Conflict theory focuses on inequality and resource distribution.
choiceExplanations.B: Symbolic interactionism focuses more on meanings created through microlevel interactions, not primarily on structural competition over resources.
choiceExplanations.C: Psychophysics concerns sensory detection and perception, not sociological inequality.
choiceExplanations.D: Information-processing theory concerns cognition, not distribution of neighborhood resources.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing symbolic interactionism because the topic involves perceptions, while the stem asks about structural inequality and resource competition.

questions.stem: The researchers hypothesize that recreational-space use partly explains the association between neighborhood resources and stress. In this hypothesis, recreational-space use is best described as a:
questions.explanation: A mediator is a variable through which an independent variable may influence an outcome. The passage's conceptual model suggests neighborhood resources may affect stress partly by shaping opportunities for exercise, social interaction, and perceived control through recreational-space use.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Recreational-space use is proposed as part of the pathway linking resources to stress.
choiceExplanations.B: A placebo is an inert treatment or condition used as a comparison, which is not described.
choiceExplanations.C: Recreational-space use is measured, but the hypothesis gives it a pathway role rather than treating it only as an endpoint.
choiceExplanations.D: Randomization is an assignment procedure, not a measured explanatory pathway.

questions.commonMistake: Labeling every measured variable as a dependent variable instead of identifying its hypothesized role in a causal pathway.

questions.stem: Which study modification would best strengthen causal inference about whether increased access to recreational spaces reduces stress?

choices.text: Randomly assign comparable neighborhoods to receive new recreational-space improvements at different times and measure stress before and after implementation.
Measure perceived stress once in a larger sample from the same three neighborhoods.
Exclude cortisol measurements and rely only on self-reported neighborhood satisfaction.
Recruit only participants who already use recreational spaces daily.

questions.explanation: Randomly assigning comparable neighborhoods to receive improvements at different times creates a stronger quasi-experimental or experimental basis for causal inference, especially with pre-post measurement. The other options do not adequately address confounding or temporal order.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Random assignment and pre-post measurement strengthen causal inference.
choiceExplanations.B: A larger cross-sectional sample may improve precision but does not solve the causal limitation.
choiceExplanations.C: Removing physiological measurement would reduce measurement breadth and not improve causality.
choiceExplanations.D: Recruiting only daily users restricts the sample and prevents evaluating changes in access for nonusers.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming larger sample size alone converts an observational comparison into a causal design.

questions.stem: A participant in a reaction-time study responds faster when a warning light reliably appears 500 ms before the target than when no warning light appears. The warning light most likely improves performance by:

choices.text: Serving as a cue that helps orient attention before the target appears
Increasing the absolute threshold for detecting the target
Preventing the target from entering sensory memory

questions.explanation: A reliable warning cue can orient attention and prepare a response, reducing reaction time. It does not improve performance by raising the detection threshold or preventing sensory encoding.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. A predictive cue can improve attentional readiness.
choiceExplanations.B: Increasing an absolute threshold would make detection harder, not faster.
choiceExplanations.C: The stem does not involve sustained stimulation or adaptation.
choiceExplanations.D: Preventing sensory memory would impair detection, not improve reaction time.

questions.commonMistake: Misinterpreting a beneficial attentional cue as a change in sensory threshold.

questions.stem: A fictional study finds that people who repeatedly avoid elevators after one frightening elevator incident feel temporary relief each time they take the stairs. Over time, their elevator fear persists. The persistence of avoidance is best explained by:
questions.explanation: Avoidance is strengthened because it removes or reduces anxiety, which is an aversive state. This is negative reinforcement because behavior increases when something unpleasant is removed.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Taking the stairs is reinforced by relief from anxiety.
choiceExplanations.B: Positive punishment would add an unpleasant consequence to decrease behavior, which is not described.
choiceExplanations.C: Habituation would involve reduced response after repeated exposure; avoidance prevents such exposure.
choiceExplanations.D: Latent inhibition refers to slower conditioning to a familiar stimulus, not maintenance of avoidance through relief.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking negative reinforcement means punishment because the word negative is used.

questions.stem: In a fictional experiment, participants memorize a list of words and then immediately learn a second list of similar words. Later, memory for the first list is worse than it would have been without the second list. This effect is best described as:
questions.explanation: Retroactive interference occurs when newly learned information interferes with retrieval of previously learned information. The second list reduces later memory for the first list.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. New learning interferes with old learning.
choiceExplanations.B: Proactive interference occurs when old learning interferes with new learning, the reverse direction.
choiceExplanations.C: Iconic memory is brief visual sensory memory and does not explain list interference.
choiceExplanations.D: Priming is facilitated processing due to prior exposure, not impaired recall due to similar later material.

questions.commonMistake: Reversing proactive and retroactive interference.

questions.stem: Which research question is most aligned with a symbolic interactionist approach?

choices.text: How do patients and clinicians negotiate the meaning of the label "noncompliant" during clinical conversations?
How do national income distributions predict mortality across countries?
How does cortisol bind to intracellular receptors after stress exposure?
How does random assignment reduce selection bias in experiments?

questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism focuses on how people create, interpret, and negotiate meaning through social interaction. The meaning of a label in clinician-patient interaction is a strong fit.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It examines meaning-making in interaction.
choiceExplanations.B: This is a macrosociological question about population-level inequality and health.
choiceExplanations.C: This is a biological question about hormone signaling, not social meaning.
choiceExplanations.D: This is a methods question about study design rather than symbolic interaction.

questions.commonMistake: Associating symbolic interactionism with any social topic, rather than specifically with interaction-based meaning-making.

questions.stem: A fictional patient has trouble recognizing emotional facial expressions after damage to a medial temporal lobe structure involved in threat processing. Which structure is most likely affected?
questions.explanation: The amygdala is involved in emotional processing, especially threat and fear-related learning, and is located in the medial temporal lobe. Damage can affect recognition of emotional facial expressions.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The amygdala is the medial temporal structure most linked to threat and emotional expression processing.
choiceExplanations.B: The cerebellum is primarily associated with coordination and motor learning, not the described emotional recognition deficit.
choiceExplanations.C: Primary motor cortex controls voluntary movement rather than threat-related facial emotion recognition.
choiceExplanations.D: Broca's area is involved in speech production, not primarily emotional facial expression recognition.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing hippocampus-like memory structures generally rather than the amygdala's specific role in emotional threat processing.

questions.stem: During acquisition, Tone A is best described as the:
questions.explanation: Tone A was initially neutral but was repeatedly presented before an unpleasant burst of white noise. Through pairing, Tone A became a conditioned stimulus capable of producing increased autonomic arousal.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Tone A acquired predictive value through pairing with the white noise.
choiceExplanations.B: The unconditioned response would be the natural arousal response to the unpleasant white noise, not Tone A.
choiceExplanations.C: A negative reinforcer strengthens behavior by removing an aversive stimulus; Tone A is a cue, not a removed consequence.
choiceExplanations.D: Extinction is a process in which the conditioned stimulus is presented without the unconditioned stimulus, not the label for Tone A during acquisition.

questions.commonMistake: Labeling the unpleasant white noise and the predictive tone with the same conditioning term.

questions.stem: Which result in Table 1 provides the strongest evidence that conditioning occurred during acquisition?

choices.text: Late acquisition response was higher to Tone A than to Tone B.
Late extinction response to Tone B was 0.20.
Final test response to Tone C was lower than late acquisition response to Tone A.
Tone B was presented equally often as Tone A.

questions.explanation: Conditioning is shown by a larger response to the stimulus paired with the unpleasant white noise than to the unpaired control stimulus. Tone A produced a mean response of 0.78, compared with 0.22 for Tone B during late acquisition.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. A higher response to the paired tone than the unpaired tone indicates learned association.
choiceExplanations.B: A low response to Tone B during extinction does not by itself demonstrate acquisition.
choiceExplanations.C: This relates more to generalization and reduced responding after extinction than to acquisition itself.
choiceExplanations.D: Equal presentation frequency is a useful control, but it is a design feature rather than the observed evidence of conditioning.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing a procedural control rather than the response difference that demonstrates learning.

questions.stem: The response to Tone C during the final test is most consistent with:
questions.explanation: Tone C was similar to Tone A and produced an elevated skin conductance response relative to Tone B. Responding to a stimulus similar to the conditioned stimulus is stimulus generalization.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Tone C resembles Tone A and evokes a conditioned-like response.
choiceExplanations.B: Negative punishment involves removing a desirable consequence to reduce behavior, not responding to a similar sensory cue.
choiceExplanations.C: Conservation is a cognitive-developmental concept unrelated to conditioned arousal.
choiceExplanations.D: Retroactive interference concerns new learning impairing old memory, not generalization of conditioned responses.

questions.commonMistake: Treating all reduced responses after acquisition as extinction, even when the question asks about responding to a similar new stimulus.

questions.stem: Which physiological system is most directly reflected by skin conductance responses in this study?
questions.explanation: Skin conductance is commonly used as an index of autonomic arousal because sweat gland activity changes electrical conductance at the skin. It is not primarily a measure of voluntary movement or visual receptor adaptation.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Skin conductance reflects autonomic arousal, especially sympathetic activity.
choiceExplanations.B: Skeletal muscle contraction is measured more directly by motor behavior or electromyography, not skin conductance.
choiceExplanations.C: Declarative memory storage is not directly measured by skin conductance.
choiceExplanations.D: Photoreceptor adaptation occurs in the visual system and is unrelated to the skin conductance measure.

questions.commonMistake: Treating any physiological measure as a direct measure of conscious memory rather than identifying the relevant autonomic process.

questions.stem: Why was Tone B important to the design of the study?

choices.text: It served as an unpaired comparison stimulus to help show that elevated responding was specific to Tone A.
It served as the unpleasant unconditioned stimulus.
It guaranteed that extinction could not occur.
It removed the need to measure skin conductance.

questions.explanation: Tone B was presented equally often but was never followed by the unpleasant noise. This makes it a useful control stimulus, helping distinguish learning specific to the Tone A-noise pairing from general reactivity to any tone.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Tone B controls for responses to tones that are not paired with the aversive event.
choiceExplanations.B: The unpleasant burst of white noise, not Tone B, served as the unconditioned stimulus.
choiceExplanations.C: Tone B does not prevent extinction; extinction occurs when conditioned cues are presented without the unconditioned stimulus.
choiceExplanations.D: Tone B is part of the stimulus design, but physiological measurement is still needed to assess response.

questions.commonMistake: Overlooking the importance of a control stimulus in distinguishing specific conditioning from general arousal.

questions.stem: A student evaluates a study showing that a new mindfulness app is associated with lower stress among users. Users chose whether to download the app. Which improvement would best address selection bias?

choices.text: Randomly assign interested participants to app access or a comparison condition.
Advertise the study using brighter colors.
Measure stress only after app users have completed the program.
Exclude all participants who report high baseline stress.

questions.explanation: Selection bias is likely if app users differ systematically from nonusers before the intervention. Random assignment to app access or comparison condition helps create more equivalent groups.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Random assignment directly addresses preexisting group differences.
choiceExplanations.B: Advertising design does not address whether app users differ from nonusers.
choiceExplanations.C: Measuring only after app use prevents evaluating baseline differences and change over time.
choiceExplanations.D: Excluding high-stress participants reduces generalizability and does not solve selection bias among remaining participants.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming a larger or more attractive recruitment effort eliminates systematic differences between self-selected users and nonusers.

questions.stem: A person remembers how to ride a bicycle after many years without practice but cannot recall the name of the person who taught them. The preserved bicycle skill is primarily an example of:
questions.explanation: Procedural memory stores skills and habits, such as riding a bicycle. Remembering the teacher's name would involve declarative memory rather than the motor skill itself.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Bicycle riding is a learned motor skill.
choiceExplanations.B: Episodic memory concerns personal events and experiences, such as a specific lesson.
choiceExplanations.C: Semantic memory concerns facts and general knowledge, not a motor skill.
choiceExplanations.D: Echoic memory is brief auditory sensory memory.

questions.commonMistake: Classifying all long-lasting memories as declarative memories rather than distinguishing skill memory from factual or episodic memory.

questions.stem: A fictional brain-imaging study finds increased activity in the prefrontal cortex while participants suppress an automatic but incorrect response on a Stroop-like task. This finding most directly relates to:
questions.explanation: The prefrontal cortex is strongly involved in executive functions such as inhibitory control, working memory, and flexible rule use. Suppressing an automatic incorrect response on a Stroop-like task requires executive control.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Inhibiting an automatic response is an executive-control demand.
choiceExplanations.B: Phototransduction occurs in retinal photoreceptors and does not explain prefrontal activation during response inhibition.
choiceExplanations.C: Spinal reflex integration involves spinal circuits rather than higher-order cognitive control.
choiceExplanations.D: Auditory transduction occurs in the cochlea, not primarily in the prefrontal cortex.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming any task involving stimuli is mainly sensory rather than recognizing the executive demand in inhibiting an automatic response.

questions.stem: A public health researcher describes a socioeconomic gradient in health. Which pattern would best illustrate this concept?

choices.text: Each step down in income or education is associated with progressively worse average health outcomes.
Only the very poorest group differs from all other groups in health outcomes.
Health outcomes are unrelated to social position after childhood.
Social class affects cultural tastes but cannot affect health.

questions.explanation: A socioeconomic gradient in health means health outcomes tend to vary progressively across levels of socioeconomic status, not only between the poorest group and everyone else.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The gradient is a stepwise relationship across the social hierarchy.
choiceExplanations.B: This describes a threshold difference, not a gradient across multiple levels.
choiceExplanations.C: The gradient concept specifically asserts that social position is related to health.
choiceExplanations.D: Social class can influence health through resources, exposure, stress, and access to care.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting inequality in health as only a poverty-versus-nonpoverty contrast rather than a graded relationship.

questions.stem: A fictional individual hears voices when no external speaker is present and holds a fixed belief that coworkers are secretly broadcasting thoughts through office lights despite contrary evidence. These symptoms are most characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Hallucinations and delusions are psychosis-related symptoms. The stem is a fictional educational example and does not provide a clinical diagnosis.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Hearing voices without an external source and holding fixed false beliefs are psychosis-related symptoms.
choiceExplanations.B: Specific phobia involves marked fear of a specific object or situation, not hallucinations and delusions.
choiceExplanations.C: Sensory adaptation is reduced responsiveness to sustained stimulation, not perception without an external stimulus.
choiceExplanations.D: Procedural learning concerns skills and habits, not psychotic symptoms.

questions.commonMistake: Classifying unusual perceptual experiences as ordinary sensory phenomena rather than distinguishing hallucinations and delusions.

questions.stem: Which feature of the study most supports the claim that differences in post-feedback ratings were caused by the writing prompt manipulation?

choices.text: Students were randomly assigned to the writing prompt conditions.
Students completed a difficult reasoning task.
Students rated willingness on a 1 to 7 scale.

questions.explanation: Random assignment helps make the groups comparable before the manipulation, supporting a causal interpretation of differences after feedback. The other features describe the task, sample, or measurement scale but do not support causality as directly.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Random assignment is the key causal design feature.
choiceExplanations.B: The difficult task helps create a feedback situation but does not control preexisting group differences.
choiceExplanations.C: The sample describes who participated, not whether the prompt caused rating differences.
choiceExplanations.D: A rating scale measures an outcome but does not establish causality.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing an outcome measurement method with a design feature that supports causal inference.

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, which statement best describes the effect of the group-comparison prompt relative to the personal-values prompt?

choices.text: It was associated with higher perceived threat and lower willingness to attempt a similar task.
It was associated with lower perceived threat and higher expected future performance.
It produced identical ratings across all outcomes.
It increased expected future performance more than willingness.

questions.explanation: The group-comparison prompt had higher perceived threat than the personal-values prompt, 4.8 versus 3.0, and lower willingness to attempt a similar task, 4.1 versus 5.5. It also had lower expected future performance.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It matches the observed direction of the ratings.
choiceExplanations.B: This reverses the pattern shown in the table.
choiceExplanations.C: The ratings differ across conditions for all three outcomes.
choiceExplanations.D: Expected future performance was lower, not higher, in the group-comparison condition.

questions.commonMistake: Reversing which condition had higher threat after negative feedback.

questions.stem: The group-comparison prompt most likely increased the salience of which aspect of self-concept?
questions.explanation: Social identity refers to the portion of self-concept derived from group memberships. Writing about how one's school group is viewed by another school group makes group membership salient.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The prompt explicitly concerns group membership and intergroup perception.
choiceExplanations.B: A sensory threshold concerns stimulus detection, not self-concept.
choiceExplanations.C: Procedural memory concerns learned skills and habits, not group-based self-definition.
choiceExplanations.D: HPA-axis feedback is a stress physiology mechanism, not the self-concept element manipulated by the prompt.

questions.commonMistake: Treating any group-related prompt as merely stress-related without identifying the social identity manipulation.

questions.stem: Which interpretation would be most cautious and scientifically appropriate based on the passage?

choices.text: The writing prompt changed students' immediate reactions to standardized negative feedback in this study.
The group-comparison prompt permanently lowered students' academic ability.
Personal-values writing eliminates all stress responses to academic feedback.
Group identity has no effect on cognition or motivation.

questions.explanation: The study measured immediate ratings after a specific prompt and feedback manipulation. The data support differences in immediate reactions, not permanent changes in ability or complete elimination of stress.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It stays close to the design and measured outcomes.
choiceExplanations.B: The study measured expected performance, not actual permanent academic ability.
choiceExplanations.C: The personal-values condition had lower threat than the comparison condition but did not eliminate all stress.
choiceExplanations.D: The passage data suggest group identity salience may affect perceived threat and motivation-related ratings.

questions.commonMistake: Overgeneralizing short-term experimental results into permanent or universal claims.

questions.stem: A symbolic interactionist interpretation of the group-comparison condition would most likely emphasize that the feedback became more threatening because:

choices.text: Its meaning was interpreted through salient group labels and anticipated judgments from others.
The written feedback directly altered photoreceptor sensitivity.
The task removed all influence of social context.
The students had already achieved identity foreclosure.

questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism emphasizes meanings formed through social interaction and interpretation. When group comparison is salient, identical feedback may be interpreted as more threatening because it is connected to group labels and expected evaluation by others.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It connects feedback meaning to social labels and perceived judgments.
choiceExplanations.B: The prompt and feedback concern social interpretation, not sensory receptor sensitivity.
choiceExplanations.C: The group-comparison prompt increased, rather than removed, social context.
choiceExplanations.D: Identity foreclosure is commitment without exploration and is not measured or required by the passage.

questions.commonMistake: Applying a broad biological or developmental label instead of using the theoretical perspective named in the stem.

questions.stem: In a psychophysics demonstration, a participant can just notice when 2 g are added to a 100 g weight. According to Weber's law, which added mass would the participant be most likely to just notice when lifting a 300 g weight?
questions.explanation: Weber's law states that the just-noticeable difference is a constant proportion of the original stimulus intensity. If 2 g is just noticeable for 100 g, the Weber fraction is 2/100 = 0.02. For 300 g, 0.02 × 300 g = 6 g.

choiceExplanations.A: This treats the difference threshold as constant rather than proportional to the starting weight.
choiceExplanations.B: This increases the added mass but does not preserve the same Weber fraction.
choiceExplanations.C: This is correct. The just-noticeable difference scales proportionally with stimulus intensity.
choiceExplanations.D: This is much larger than the proportional difference implied by Weber's law.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that the smallest detectable change is the same absolute amount at every stimulus intensity.

questions.stem: A student memorizes endocrine glands by imagining each gland placed at a specific stop along a familiar walk from home to school. During the exam, the student mentally walks the route to retrieve the list. Which memory strategy is being used?
questions.explanation: The method of loci involves linking information to familiar spatial locations and then mentally navigating those locations during retrieval. The route provides organized retrieval cues.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The student uses a familiar spatial path as a structured memory aid.
choiceExplanations.B: Maintenance rehearsal involves repeating information without necessarily adding meaningful spatial organization.
choiceExplanations.C: State-dependent retrieval involves improved recall when internal states match at encoding and retrieval, not spatial placement along a route.
choiceExplanations.D: Implicit priming involves prior exposure influencing later processing without deliberate retrieval.

questions.commonMistake: Classifying any study strategy as rehearsal instead of identifying the specific use of spatial retrieval cues.

questions.stem: A researcher believes that people who drink tea are more patient. When reviewing interview transcripts, the researcher highlights comments from tea drinkers that sound patient but overlooks impatient comments from the same participants. Which cognitive bias is most clearly illustrated?
questions.explanation: Confirmation bias occurs when people preferentially seek, notice, or interpret evidence that supports an existing belief while discounting contrary evidence. The researcher selectively attends to transcript evidence that supports the hypothesis.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The researcher gives more weight to belief-consistent evidence.
choiceExplanations.B: Functional fixedness is difficulty using an object in a novel way, not biased evidence evaluation.
choiceExplanations.C: Belief perseverance refers to maintaining a belief after it has been discredited; the stem focuses on selective evidence processing.
choiceExplanations.D: The representativeness heuristic involves judging category membership by similarity to a prototype.

questions.commonMistake: Mistaking biased interpretation of evidence for a general problem-solving limitation.

questions.stem: In a fictional educational vignette, a person experiences repeated intrusive thoughts about contamination and spends hours washing to reduce distress, despite recognizing that the behavior is excessive. This pattern is most characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Obsessions are intrusive, unwanted thoughts, and compulsions are repetitive behaviors performed to reduce distress. The vignette describes contamination obsessions and washing compulsions in a fictional educational context.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Intrusive contamination thoughts and repetitive washing align with obsessive-compulsive symptoms.
choiceExplanations.B: Dissociative amnesia involves inability to recall important autobiographical information, not repetitive washing rituals.
choiceExplanations.C: Antisocial traits involve disregard for others' rights and norms, which is not described.
choiceExplanations.D: Narcoleptic episodes involve sleep-wake disruption, not intrusive thoughts and compulsive rituals.

questions.commonMistake: Focusing only on anxiety and missing the combination of intrusive thoughts and repetitive rituals.

questions.stem: During an acute startle response, adrenal medulla secretion rapidly increases circulating epinephrine and norepinephrine. This response is most closely associated with which pathway?

choices.text: Melatonin release from the pineal gland
Insulin release from pancreatic beta cells after a meal
Thyroxine suppression by the auditory cortex

questions.explanation: Acute stress can activate the sympathetic nervous system and adrenal medulla, leading to rapid release of catecholamines such as epinephrine and norepinephrine. This supports immediate physiological arousal.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The adrenal medulla is part of the rapid sympathetic-adrenal-medullary stress response.
choiceExplanations.B: Melatonin is involved in circadian regulation and is not the rapid catecholamine response described.
choiceExplanations.C: Insulin regulates blood glucose after nutrient intake rather than mediating the startle response.
choiceExplanations.D: The auditory cortex does not directly suppress thyroxine in the described pathway.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing slower endocrine regulatory systems with the rapid catecholamine response to acute stress.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by Table 1?

choices.text: Ambient scent influenced taste-related ratings even though the beverage was identical across conditions.
The coffee scent increased purchase intention more than the lemon scent.
Participants could not distinguish sweetness from sourness.
The no-scent condition produced the highest perceived sweetness.

questions.explanation: The passage states that the beverage was identical across conditions, yet ratings varied. The congruent lemon scent produced higher sweetness and purchase intention than the other conditions, supporting a multisensory influence on perception and choice.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The same beverage received different ratings depending on ambient odor.
choiceExplanations.B: The coffee scent produced lower purchase intention than the lemon scent.
choiceExplanations.C: The table shows separate sweetness and sourness ratings, but it does not show that participants could not distinguish them.
choiceExplanations.D: The congruent lemon scent produced the highest perceived sweetness.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that identical physical stimuli must produce identical perceptual ratings.

questions.stem: The finding that odor context changed sweetness judgments most directly illustrates:
questions.explanation: Multisensory integration occurs when information from different sensory modalities combines to shape perception. Here, smell influenced taste-related judgments of the same beverage.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Olfactory context influenced gustatory perception.
choiceExplanations.B: Operant extinction concerns decreased behavior after reinforcement is removed, which is not the perceptual process tested.
choiceExplanations.C: Intergenerational mobility concerns class movement across generations and is unrelated to beverage perception.
choiceExplanations.D: Negative symptoms of psychosis involve reductions in normal functioning, not odor-taste interaction.

questions.commonMistake: Treating smell and taste as completely separate systems when perceptual judgments often combine multiple sensory inputs.

questions.stem: The increase in lemon odor detection threshold immediately after continuous odor exposure in Table 2 is best explained by:
questions.explanation: After continuous exposure to the lemon scent, participants needed a higher odor concentration to detect it. Reduced sensitivity after sustained stimulation is sensory adaptation.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Sustained exposure reduced sensitivity to that odor.
choiceExplanations.B: Long-term potentiation is a neural mechanism related to synaptic strengthening, not the immediate threshold shift shown here.
choiceExplanations.C: Social loafing involves reduced individual effort in groups, not odor detection.
choiceExplanations.D: Role conflict involves incompatible demands from different social roles.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting a higher detection threshold as improved sensitivity rather than reduced sensitivity.

questions.stem: Why was counterbalancing the beverage scent conditions useful in this study?

choices.text: It reduced the likelihood that condition order alone explained the rating differences.
It made the beverage chemically different across scent conditions.
It removed the need for participant ratings.
It guaranteed that participants could not experience sensory adaptation.

questions.explanation: Counterbalancing distributes order effects across conditions. Because each participant experienced all scent conditions, counterbalancing helps prevent fatigue, practice, or carryover from being confounded with a specific scent condition.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Counterbalancing addresses order as an alternative explanation.
choiceExplanations.B: The passage states that the same beverage was used across conditions.
choiceExplanations.C: Participant ratings were still needed as dependent measures.
choiceExplanations.D: Counterbalancing reduces order confounds but does not make adaptation impossible.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing counterbalancing with blinding or with changing the independent variable itself.

questions.stem: Which additional procedure would best reduce demand characteristics in the beverage-rating portion of the study?

choices.text: Use a cover story that plausibly masks the purpose of the ambient scent manipulation.
Tell participants that lemon scent is expected to increase sweetness ratings.
Present only the congruent lemon scent condition to all participants.

questions.explanation: Demand characteristics occur when participants infer the study hypothesis and alter behavior accordingly. A plausible cover story or masking procedure would reduce the chance that participants intentionally adjust ratings to match perceived expectations.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Masking the study purpose can reduce hypothesis guessing.
choiceExplanations.B: This would increase demand characteristics by revealing the expected effect.
choiceExplanations.C: Using only one scent condition would eliminate key comparisons.
choiceExplanations.D: Removing the no-scent condition would weaken the design by eliminating a baseline comparison.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that participants' lack of explicit instruction about scent fully eliminates expectation effects.

questions.stem: A person whose parents worked hourly service jobs completes graduate training and enters a high-paying professional occupation. In sociology, this change is best described as:

choices.text: Horizontal mobility within the same class position
