questions.explanation: Intergenerational mobility compares social position across generations. Movement from parents' lower-status occupations to a higher-paying professional occupation is upward intergenerational mobility.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The comparison is between generations and the direction is upward.
choiceExplanations.B: Downward intragenerational mobility would refer to decline within the same person's lifetime.
choiceExplanations.C: Horizontal mobility involves changing roles or jobs without substantial class movement.
choiceExplanations.D: Role exit involves leaving a social role, not necessarily moving across class positions.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing intergenerational mobility with mobility that occurs within one person's career.

questions.stem: A student is expected to attend a required evening laboratory session at the same time they are expected to care for a younger sibling at home. Which concept best describes the tension between these expectations?
questions.explanation: Role conflict occurs when expectations from different social roles are incompatible. The student role and family caregiver role impose conflicting demands at the same time.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The tension is between two distinct roles.
choiceExplanations.B: Role strain occurs when demands within a single role are difficult to meet.
choiceExplanations.C: Status consistency refers to alignment among social status indicators, such as income and education.
choiceExplanations.D: Anomie refers to normlessness or weakened social regulation, not incompatible role expectations.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing role strain whenever a role is stressful, rather than identifying whether the conflict is within one role or between roles.

questions.stem: Damage to hair cells in the cochlea would most directly impair which process?

choices.text: Transduction of mechanical sound vibrations into neural signals
Secretion of cortisol after social stress
Encoding of autobiographical events in the hippocampus

questions.explanation: Cochlear hair cells convert mechanical vibrations caused by sound into neural signals sent through the auditory pathway. Damage to these cells can impair hearing.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Cochlear hair cells are sensory receptors for auditory transduction.
choiceExplanations.B: Cortisol secretion involves endocrine stress pathways rather than cochlear receptors.
choiceExplanations.C: Autobiographical event encoding involves memory systems, not cochlear hair cells.
choiceExplanations.D: Class-based social networks are sociological phenomena rather than sensory transduction processes.

questions.commonMistake: Treating sensory receptors as passive structures rather than recognizing their role in transduction.

questions.stem: A participant hears a 20-word list once and immediately recalls mostly the first few and last few words. Which pattern is being demonstrated?
questions.explanation: The serial position effect refers to better recall of items at the beginning and end of a list. The beginning reflects a primacy component, and the end reflects a recency component.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The recall pattern favors early and late list items.
choiceExplanations.B: Opponent-process theory explains aspects of color vision and some motivational states, not list-position memory patterns.
choiceExplanations.C: Fundamental attribution error concerns explaining others' behavior by dispositions rather than situations.
choiceExplanations.D: Habituation is decreased response to repeated stimulation.

questions.commonMistake: Labeling any memory advantage as priming instead of recognizing the position-based recall pattern.

questions.stem: During a problem-solving task, a participant cannot think to use a binder clip as a temporary zipper pull because they think of it only as a device for holding papers together. This difficulty best illustrates:
questions.explanation: Functional fixedness is a cognitive bias that limits problem solving by making it difficult to see objects as having uses beyond their typical functions.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The participant is constrained by the object's usual function.
choiceExplanations.B: Divided attention concerns processing multiple tasks or stimuli at once, not generating alternate object uses.
choiceExplanations.C: Sensory accommodation is not the relevant problem-solving concept here.
choiceExplanations.D: Out-group homogeneity involves perceiving members of another group as more similar to one another.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing a barrier to creative use of objects with a limitation in sensory attention.

questions.stem: Which interpretation is best supported by the data in Table 1?

choices.text: Challenge framing was associated with lower cortisol increase and better working-memory accuracy than threat framing during the same speech task.
Challenge framing eliminated all physiological arousal during the speech task.
The private-reading group experienced the strongest stress response.
Threat and challenge framing produced identical perceived control ratings.

questions.explanation: The challenge and threat groups completed the same speech task but differed in framing. Compared with threat framing, challenge framing showed lower cortisol increase, higher perceived control, and higher working-memory accuracy, while heart rate still increased substantially.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The table supports a difference between challenge and threat framing on cortisol and working memory.
choiceExplanations.B: The challenge group still showed a 16 beats/min heart rate increase and a cortisol increase.
choiceExplanations.C: The private-reading group had the lowest physiological changes.
choiceExplanations.D: Perceived control was higher in the challenge group than in the threat group.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that lower cortisol means no arousal occurred, despite the heart-rate data.

questions.stem: The challenge framing most directly targeted participants' appraisal of:

choices.text: Whether their resources could help them meet the task demands
Whether the speech sounds entered the cochlea
Whether the task used a fixed-ratio reinforcement schedule
Whether social class is inherited genetically

questions.explanation: Stress appraisal involves evaluating a situation's demands and one's resources for coping with those demands. The challenge statement reframed arousal as potentially useful, increasing perceived control and resource appraisal.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Challenge appraisal emphasizes the possibility that available resources can meet demands.
choiceExplanations.B: The framing did not manipulate auditory transduction.
choiceExplanations.C: No reinforcement schedule was used in the speech task.
choiceExplanations.D: The passage does not concern genetic inheritance of class.

questions.commonMistake: Reducing stress appraisal to stimulus exposure alone rather than considering interpretation of demands and coping resources.

questions.stem: Which feature of the study most supports causal inference about the effect of appraisal framing?

choices.text: Participants were randomly assigned to the three groups.
The private-reading task involved a neutral passage.
Working memory was measured after the task.
Heart rate was reported in beats per minute.

questions.explanation: Random assignment helps distribute preexisting differences across conditions, strengthening the claim that differences in outcomes were caused by the task and framing manipulations.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Random assignment is the key design feature for causal inference.
choiceExplanations.B: A neutral reading task provides a comparison condition but does not by itself ensure equivalent groups.
choiceExplanations.C: Post-task measurement establishes timing but does not control selection differences as directly as random assignment.
choiceExplanations.D: Measurement units do not address causal inference.

questions.commonMistake: Selecting a precise measurement detail rather than the design feature that reduces selection differences.

questions.stem: Which result would most directly support the idea that reduced perceived control helped explain the working-memory impairment in the evaluative-threat group?

choices.text: Across participants, lower perceived control statistically predicts lower working-memory accuracy even after accounting for task condition.
The private-reading group has the smallest heart rate increase.
All participants report that they understood the instructions.
The speech task lasts the same number of minutes in both evaluative groups.

questions.explanation: If perceived control is part of the pathway linking threat framing to working-memory performance, then lower control should be associated with lower accuracy even when task condition is considered. This would support, though not prove by itself, a mediating role for perceived control.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It directly links the proposed psychological mediator to the cognitive outcome.
choiceExplanations.B: This describes a physiological difference for the comparison group but does not test perceived control as an explanatory pathway.
choiceExplanations.C: Instruction comprehension is useful but does not connect control appraisal to working memory.
choiceExplanations.D: Equal task duration is a procedural control but not evidence for the proposed mediator.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing a procedural similarity instead of evidence connecting the proposed mediator to the outcome.

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, what is the approximate difference in working-memory accuracy between the evaluative-challenge and evaluative-threat groups?
questions.explanation: The evaluative-challenge group had 79% working-memory accuracy, and the evaluative-threat group had 71% accuracy. The difference is 79 minus 71, or 8 percentage points.

choiceExplanations.A: This is closer to the heart-rate difference between the two evaluative groups, not the accuracy difference.
choiceExplanations.B: This is correct. The working-memory accuracy difference is 8 percentage points.
choiceExplanations.C: This is the difference between private reading and threat accuracy, not challenge and threat accuracy.
choiceExplanations.D: This is the heart-rate increase in the threat group, not an accuracy difference.

questions.commonMistake: Using the wrong column or comparing the threat group with private reading instead of challenge framing.

questions.stem: A person remembers reading a surprising fact but later incorrectly claims that a close friend told it to them. Which memory error is most directly illustrated?
questions.explanation: Source monitoring involves identifying where a memory came from. Misattributing a fact learned from reading to a conversation with a friend is a source monitoring error.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The content is remembered, but the source is misidentified.
choiceExplanations.B: Sensory adaptation is reduced responsiveness after sustained stimulation.
choiceExplanations.C: Formal operational thought involves abstract reasoning, not source attribution.
choiceExplanations.D: Negative reinforcement strengthens behavior by removing an aversive stimulus.

questions.commonMistake: Treating every memory error as forgetting rather than distinguishing content retrieval from source attribution.

questions.stem: In a fictional animal-learning study, animals first experience unavoidable mild stressors. Later, when escape becomes possible, many do not attempt to escape. This pattern is most consistent with:
questions.explanation: Learned helplessness can occur when exposure to uncontrollable aversive events leads an organism to stop trying to avoid or escape even when control becomes possible.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Prior uncontrollability reduces later escape attempts.
choiceExplanations.B: Stimulus discrimination involves responding differently to distinct cues.
choiceExplanations.C: Observational modeling involves learning by watching another individual.
choiceExplanations.D: Latent learning refers to learning that is not immediately expressed until motivation or reinforcement changes.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that failure to act reflects lack of ability rather than a learned expectation of uncontrollability.

questions.stem: A sociologist argues that what counts as a normal transition to adulthood differs across cultures and historical periods because societies assign different meanings to work, marriage, education, and independence. This argument most closely reflects:
questions.explanation: Social constructionism emphasizes that categories and meanings are created and maintained through social processes. The idea that adulthood is defined differently across cultures and time periods fits this perspective.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The concept of adulthood is treated as socially defined rather than purely biological.
choiceExplanations.B: Vestibular transduction concerns balance and motion sensation.
choiceExplanations.C: Fixed action patterns are innate behavioral sequences and do not explain culturally variable meanings.
choiceExplanations.D: Punishment generalization is not the concept described.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming age categories are purely biological and overlooking socially assigned meanings.

questions.stem: A neuroscience study finds that unexpected rewards produce increased firing in midbrain neurons that project to the nucleus accumbens. Which neurotransmitter system is most strongly implicated?
questions.explanation: Dopaminergic pathways from the midbrain to reward-related regions such as the nucleus accumbens are strongly involved in reward prediction and reinforcement learning.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The mesolimbic dopamine system is central to reward processing.
choiceExplanations.B: Melatonin is primarily associated with circadian rhythms.
choiceExplanations.C: Histamine has roles in arousal and immune responses but is not the primary reward-prediction system described.
choiceExplanations.D: Oxytocin is associated with social bonding and other functions but is not the main midbrain-to-nucleus accumbens reward signal described.

questions.commonMistake: Associating reward with any hormone or neurotransmitter rather than identifying the mesolimbic dopamine pathway.

questions.stem: A student who is overwhelmed by multiple deadlines creates a schedule, asks for one deadline extension, and divides the work into smaller tasks. This coping response is best classified as:
questions.explanation: Problem-focused coping attempts to change the stressor or one's practical relationship to it. Scheduling, requesting an extension, and breaking tasks into parts directly address the demands.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The student is taking actions to manage the source of stress.
choiceExplanations.B: Emotion-focused coping regulates emotional distress; the stem emphasizes practical changes to the stressor.
choiceExplanations.C: Classical conditioning involves associating stimuli, not planning around deadlines.
choiceExplanations.D: Identity diffusion involves lack of exploration and commitment, not coping with deadlines.

questions.commonMistake: Classifying any stress-management behavior as emotion-focused without asking whether it changes the stressor.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by Table 1?

choices.text: Workshop attendance was associated with higher four-week comprehension scores at all three centers, but resource differences across districts remained visible.
The workshop fully eliminated baseline differences among districts.
Nonattendees in the working-class district outperformed attendees in the professional-class district.
Broadband access was identical across the three districts.

questions.explanation: Workshop attendees had higher four-week scores than nonattendees in every district. However, the professional-class district still had the highest scores and greatest broadband access, showing persistent resource differences.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The table supports both workshop-associated gains and continuing district differences.
choiceExplanations.B: Four-week scores still differed by district, so baseline differences were not fully eliminated.
choiceExplanations.C: Working-class nonattendees scored 60, whereas professional-class attendees scored 82.
choiceExplanations.D: Broadband access ranged from 62% to 94%, so it was not identical.

questions.commonMistake: Overstating the workshop effect by ignoring remaining differences in scores and resources across districts.

questions.stem: Which limitation most directly affects causal claims about the workshop's effect on comprehension?

choices.text: Participants chose whether to attend the workshop.
The comprehension test was scored out of 100.
The study included more than one education center.

questions.explanation: Because attendance was optional, attendees may have differed from nonattendees in motivation, time availability, baseline skills, or access to resources. This selection issue limits causal inference.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Self-selection into attendance creates a plausible confound.
choiceExplanations.B: A 100-point scale is a measurement format, not the key causal limitation.
choiceExplanations.C: Including multiple centers can improve external relevance and does not itself weaken causality.
choiceExplanations.D: Measuring broadband access provides contextual information and does not create the main causal limitation.

questions.commonMistake: Treating a clear score scale as a methodological weakness while missing self-selection into the intervention.

questions.stem: The differences in reliable broadband access across districts most directly relate to which concept?
questions.explanation: The digital divide refers to unequal access to digital technologies and internet resources. The table shows lower reliable broadband access in the working-class district than in the professional-class district.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Broadband inequality is a digital divide issue.
choiceExplanations.B: Binocular disparity is a depth cue based on slightly different retinal images.
choiceExplanations.C: Spontaneous recovery is reappearance of a conditioned response after a rest period.
choiceExplanations.D: State-dependent memory concerns matching internal states at encoding and retrieval.

questions.commonMistake: Viewing internet access as a purely individual preference rather than a stratified resource.

questions.stem: Which interpretation of the workshop is most consistent with functionalism?

choices.text: The workshop helps an institution teach skills that support informed participation in social systems.
The workshop matters only because all health categories are meaningless.
The workshop proves that class resources have no effect on learning.
The workshop is unrelated to any social institution.

questions.explanation: Functionalism emphasizes how social institutions contribute to social stability and functioning. An adult education center teaching health-information skills can be interpreted as helping individuals participate effectively in health and civic systems.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It frames the institution as serving a social function.
choiceExplanations.B: Functionalism does not claim that categories are meaningless.
choiceExplanations.C: The passage shows resource differences across districts, so this claim is not supported.
choiceExplanations.D: Adult education centers are social institutions, and the workshop is institutional activity.

questions.commonMistake: Equating all sociological analysis of institutions with inequality critique rather than matching the functionalist emphasis on social functions.

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, what is the difference between four-week scores for workshop attendees in the professional-class district and workshop attendees in the working-class district?
questions.explanation: Workshop attendees in the professional-class district scored 82, and attendees in the working-class district scored 72. The difference is 82 minus 72, or 10 points.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the difference between mixed-income and professional-class attendee scores, not working-class and professional-class attendee scores.
choiceExplanations.B: This is correct. The score gap is 10 points.
choiceExplanations.C: This is the improvement among working-class workshop attendees from baseline to follow-up, not the cross-district attendee gap.
choiceExplanations.D: This is the difference between professional-class attendees and working-class nonattendees.

questions.commonMistake: Calculating an improvement within one district when the question asks for a difference between two districts among attendees.

questions.stem: A person judges depth partly because each eye receives a slightly different image of the same object. This depth cue is called:
questions.explanation: Retinal disparity is a binocular depth cue created by the slightly different images projected onto the two retinas. The visual system uses this difference to infer depth.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It describes a binocular cue based on differences between the two retinal images.
choiceExplanations.B: A Weber fraction concerns the proportional change needed to detect a stimulus difference.
choiceExplanations.C: Olfactory adaptation concerns reduced smell sensitivity after sustained odor exposure.
choiceExplanations.D: Auditory shadowing is an attention task involving repeated speech input, not binocular depth perception.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing binocular depth cues with general stimulus-detection principles.

questions.stem: A person solving a logic puzzle tests every possible arrangement systematically until one satisfies all constraints. This strategy is best described as:
questions.explanation: An algorithm is a systematic, step-by-step procedure that can guarantee a solution if applied correctly. Testing every possible arrangement is algorithmic problem solving.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The strategy is exhaustive and rule-based.
choiceExplanations.B: A heuristic is a mental shortcut that may be efficient but does not guarantee a solution.
choiceExplanations.C: Sensitization is increased response after repeated or intense stimulation.
choiceExplanations.D: Social facilitation concerns performance changes due to the presence of others.

questions.commonMistake: Calling any problem-solving method a heuristic without considering whether the method is exhaustive and guaranteed.

questions.stem: In a fictional educational case, a person shows greatly reduced emotional expression, limited speech, and decreased motivation. These are best classified as:
questions.explanation: Negative symptoms involve reductions or losses of typical functions, such as reduced emotional expression, reduced speech, and decreased motivation. The vignette is educational and not a diagnosis.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The symptoms reflect diminished normal functioning.
choiceExplanations.B: Manic symptoms typically involve elevated or irritable mood, increased energy, and decreased need for sleep.
choiceExplanations.C: Panic symptoms involve sudden episodes of intense fear with physiological arousal.
choiceExplanations.D: Somatic reflexes are automatic motor responses, not a psychiatric symptom category.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking negative symptoms mean distressing or undesirable symptoms rather than reductions in normal behavior or affect.

questions.stem: An economic recession causes many workers in a manufacturing region to lose stable jobs and move into lower-paying temporary work. This pattern is best described as:

choices.text: Exchange mobility with no class change

questions.explanation: Structural mobility occurs when broader economic or social changes shift many people's class positions. A recession causing widespread movement into lower-paying temporary work is structural downward mobility.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The cause is a broad economic change and the direction is downward.
choiceExplanations.B: Meritocratic upward mobility would involve rising status through achievement, not recession-driven decline.
choiceExplanations.C: Exchange mobility involves individuals trading places in the hierarchy without changing the overall distribution.
choiceExplanations.D: Primary socialization occurs early in life through family and close caregivers.

questions.commonMistake: Explaining group-level economic mobility entirely by individual choices rather than structural change.

questions.stem: A participant kept in a room with shifting light exposure begins sleeping and waking at unusual times. Disruption of which brain structure would most directly impair synchronization of circadian rhythms to light cues?
questions.explanation: The suprachiasmatic nucleus of the hypothalamus is a key circadian pacemaker and receives light-related input that helps synchronize sleep-wake rhythms.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The suprachiasmatic nucleus coordinates circadian timing.
choiceExplanations.B: Primary somatosensory cortex processes bodily sensation, not circadian entrainment.
choiceExplanations.C: The cerebellar vermis is involved in motor coordination and posture, not the central circadian clock.
choiceExplanations.D: Broca's area is involved in speech production.

questions.commonMistake: Attributing sleep timing entirely to conscious choice rather than recognizing hypothalamic circadian regulation.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by Table 1?

choices.text: The variable-ratio points condition produced the highest Week 4 recycling accuracy and maintained more accuracy than the fixed-ratio condition after rewards were removed.
Informational signs alone produced the largest Week 4 improvement.
Fixed-ratio points maintained recycling accuracy better than observational modeling after rewards were removed.
All conditions had identical accuracy during the reward-removal week.

questions.explanation: The variable-ratio condition reached 75% accuracy at Week 4, the highest of all conditions, and remained at 63% during reward removal, compared with 50% in the fixed-ratio condition.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It accurately describes both Week 4 and reward-removal patterns.
choiceExplanations.B: Informational signs alone improved to 55%, less than the other intervention conditions at Week 4.
choiceExplanations.C: Fixed-ratio points fell to 50%, whereas observational modeling remained at 66%.
choiceExplanations.D: Reward-removal week values ranged from 50% to 66%.

questions.commonMistake: Looking only at Week 4 gains and ignoring maintenance after rewards were removed.

questions.stem: In the reward conditions, the green light indicating that points could be earned is best described as a:
questions.explanation: A discriminative stimulus signals that a particular response is likely to be reinforced. The green light indicated that correct sorting could earn points.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The light signaled the availability of reinforcement for correct sorting.
choiceExplanations.B: An unconditioned response is an automatic response to an unconditioned stimulus, not a signal for reward availability.
choiceExplanations.C: A negative punisher removes a desirable stimulus to reduce behavior; the green light did not function that way.
choiceExplanations.D: A sensory receptor detects physical stimuli; the light itself is not a receptor.

questions.commonMistake: Calling the cue a reinforcer rather than recognizing that it signals when reinforcement is available.

questions.stem: The difference between the fixed-ratio and variable-ratio reward-removal results is most consistent with which principle?

choices.text: Behavior reinforced on variable schedules is often more resistant to extinction than behavior reinforced on predictable schedules.
Predictable rewards always produce greater persistence than unpredictable rewards.
Reinforcement schedules affect only reflexes, not voluntary behavior.
Removing rewards always increases the reinforced behavior.

questions.explanation: The variable-ratio condition declined less after reward removal than the fixed-ratio condition. Variable schedules can produce persistent responding because the absence of immediate reward is less clearly informative that reinforcement has ended.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The table shows greater persistence after a variable-ratio schedule.
choiceExplanations.B: The table shows the opposite pattern during reward removal.
choiceExplanations.C: Operant reinforcement schedules can affect voluntary behavior such as recycling choices.
choiceExplanations.D: Removing rewards usually decreases reinforced behavior, although the degree of decrease can vary.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming predictable reinforcement always creates the strongest long-term behavior.

questions.stem: In this study, the primary dependent variable was:

choices.text: The number of possible answer choices on a survey

questions.explanation: The dependent variable is the measured outcome. The table reports recycling accuracy as the percentage of items correctly sorted across time and conditions.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Recycling accuracy is the outcome measured.
choiceExplanations.B: Condition assignment is an independent variable manipulated by the researchers.
choiceExplanations.C: Dormitory kitchens are part of the setting, not the measured behavioral outcome.
choiceExplanations.D: The passage does not describe a survey-answer-choice measure as the primary outcome.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing the manipulated condition with the measured behavioral outcome.

questions.stem: Because dormitory floors, not individual residents, were randomly assigned to conditions, which analytic concern is most important?

choices.text: Residents on the same floor may not provide statistically independent observations.
No baseline measure can be collected in a cluster design.
Random assignment is impossible when groups share kitchens.
The dependent variable must be a hormone measure.

questions.explanation: When groups rather than individuals are assigned, observations from individuals within the same group may be correlated because they share the same environment and intervention. Analyses should account for clustering or use the floor as the relevant unit.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Shared floor conditions can create nonindependence among residents' behaviors.
choiceExplanations.B: The study did collect baseline accuracy by condition.
choiceExplanations.C: Random assignment can occur at the group or cluster level.
choiceExplanations.D: The dependent variable can be behavioral, such as recycling accuracy.

questions.commonMistake: Analyzing clustered data as if every individual observation were fully independent.

questions.stem: A teacher assumes a quiet student is uninterested in class, even though the student is quiet because they are worried about a family emergency. The teacher's explanation most clearly illustrates:
questions.explanation: Fundamental attribution error involves overemphasizing dispositional explanations for others' behavior while underemphasizing situational factors. The teacher attributes quietness to disinterest rather than the family emergency.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The teacher overweights a dispositional explanation and underweights the situation.
choiceExplanations.B: Latent inhibition is slower conditioning to a familiar stimulus previously experienced without consequence.
choiceExplanations.C: Absolute poverty refers to lacking basic resources for survival.
choiceExplanations.D: Iconic storage is brief visual sensory memory.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that the first explanation for a behavior is accurate without considering situational constraints.

questions.stem: A cellular neuroscience experiment finds that repeated coactivation of two neurons strengthens the synaptic response between them for hours. This phenomenon is most relevant to which mechanism of memory?
questions.explanation: Long-term potentiation is a lasting increase in synaptic strength after repeated activation and is widely considered a cellular mechanism relevant to learning and memory.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Strengthened synaptic response after coactivation describes long-term potentiation.
choiceExplanations.B: Place theory explains pitch perception based on cochlear location, not synaptic strengthening.
choiceExplanations.C: Operant punishment decreases behavior through consequences, not synaptic plasticity.
choiceExplanations.D: Relative deprivation concerns perceived disadvantage compared with others.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing a cellular memory mechanism with a behavioral learning consequence.

questions.stem: A dancer spins several times and then stops but briefly feels as if the room is still moving. Which sensory system is most directly involved?
questions.explanation: The vestibular system detects head motion and balance through structures of the inner ear. After spinning stops, continued fluid motion can contribute to a lingering sensation of movement.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The sensation of rotation and balance depends heavily on vestibular input.
choiceExplanations.B: The olfactory system processes smell, not rotational motion.
choiceExplanations.C: The gustatory system processes taste.
choiceExplanations.D: Pain pathways do not directly explain the sensation of continued spinning.

questions.commonMistake: Attributing balance and motion perception only to vision rather than recognizing vestibular input.

questions.stem: A family can afford food and shelter but cannot afford the internet access, transportation, and clothing typically needed for full participation in their local community. This situation is best described as:
questions.explanation: Relative poverty refers to lacking resources needed to participate in the standard way of life in a given society, even if basic survival needs are met.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The family lacks resources relative to local participation norms.
choiceExplanations.B: Absolute threshold is a sensory detection concept, not a poverty measure.
choiceExplanations.C: Role strain is tension within one social role.
choiceExplanations.D: Primary reinforcement involves biologically important reinforcers such as food or water.

questions.commonMistake: Equating poverty only with survival deprivation and overlooking socially relative standards of participation.

questions.stem: A study uses mild deception by telling participants that a peer will evaluate their speech, although no peer evaluation will occur. Which ethical procedure is most important after participation?

choices.text: Debrief participants about the deception and the study purpose.
Withhold all information permanently to preserve the manipulation.
Publish participants' names to improve transparency.
Prevent participants from withdrawing their data.

questions.explanation: When deception is used in human-subjects research, participants should generally be debriefed afterward, including an explanation of the deception and study purpose, while protecting confidentiality and autonomy.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. Debriefing is central when deception is ethically permitted.
choiceExplanations.B: Permanently withholding the deception would violate ethical expectations for debriefing.
choiceExplanations.C: Publishing names would breach confidentiality.
choiceExplanations.D: Participants' rights and autonomy should be respected within the approved research protocol.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking methodological validity overrides participant debriefing and confidentiality obligations.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by Table 1?

choices.text: The danger-focused message produced the highest recognition rate but also the highest stigma and lowest willingness to recommend help-seeking.
The coping-continuum message produced the highest stigma rating.
The neutral message produced the highest willingness to recommend help-seeking.
All messages had identical effects on stigma.

questions.explanation: The danger-focused message had 80% recognition, the highest of all groups, but it also had the highest stigma rating, 5.2, and the lowest help-seeking willingness, 3.1.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It captures the tradeoff shown in the table.
choiceExplanations.B: The coping-continuum message had the lowest stigma rating, 2.7.
choiceExplanations.C: The neutral message had willingness of 3.9, lower than the biological and coping-continuum messages.
choiceExplanations.D: Stigma ratings varied substantially across conditions.

questions.commonMistake: Considering recognition alone as the best outcome while ignoring stigma and help-seeking measures.

questions.stem: A labeling-theory interpretation of the danger-focused message would most likely emphasize that:

choices.text: Applying stigmatizing labels can shape social reactions to the person described in the vignette.
Panic-like symptoms are caused only by retinal processing.
Random assignment prevents all possible measurement error.
Help-seeking ratings are unrelated to social meaning.

questions.explanation: Labeling theory examines how labels and social reactions can influence identity, stigma, and treatment by others. The danger-focused message may increase stigmatizing interpretations of the vignette.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It focuses on how labels affect social responses.
choiceExplanations.B: The vignette concerns psychological distress, not a retinal mechanism.
choiceExplanations.C: Random assignment improves causal inference but does not eliminate all measurement error.
choiceExplanations.D: Help-seeking attitudes can be strongly shaped by social meaning and stigma.

questions.commonMistake: Treating labels as neutral descriptions rather than recognizing their possible social consequences.

questions.stem: The vignette's sudden episodes of intense fear, rapid heartbeat, trembling, and worry about having another episode are most consistent with symptoms commonly associated with:

choices.text: A circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder only

questions.explanation: The vignette describes recurrent sudden fear episodes with physiological arousal and worry about future episodes, which is broadly consistent with panic-related symptoms. The passage is educational and does not diagnose any real person.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. The symptom pattern is most consistent with panic-related symptoms.
choiceExplanations.B: Specific learning disorder concerns difficulties in academic skills such as reading or mathematics.
choiceExplanations.C: Dissociative fugue involves unexpected travel or wandering with autobiographical memory disruption.
choiceExplanations.D: Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorders concern misalignment of sleep timing, not sudden fear episodes with autonomic arousal.

questions.commonMistake: Focusing on physical arousal alone and missing the episodic fear and anticipatory worry pattern.

questions.stem: Which modification would best test whether message effects extend beyond intentions to actual behavior?

choices.text: Measure whether participants later click a resource link or attend an optional informational session.
Remove the vignette and ask only about favorite campus buildings.
Use only the message condition with the highest stigma rating.
Stop random assignment and allow participants to select their preferred message.

questions.explanation: Actual behavior can be assessed by observing a concrete action, such as clicking a resource link or attending a session. This would complement self-reported willingness ratings.

choiceExplanations.A: This is correct. It adds a behavioral outcome related to help-seeking.
choiceExplanations.B: Favorite campus buildings would not measure mental-health help-seeking behavior.
choiceExplanations.C: Using only one condition would prevent comparing message effects.
choiceExplanations.D: Self-selection would weaken causal inference about message effects.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming stated intentions are identical to later behavior without measuring an actual action.

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, what is the difference in willingness to recommend help-seeking between the coping-continuum message and the neutral campus-services message?
questions.explanation: The coping-continuum message had a willingness rating of 5.1, and the neutral message had a willingness rating of 3.9. The difference is 5.1 minus 3.9, or 1.2 points.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the difference between the biological-explanation and neutral willingness ratings, not the coping-continuum and neutral ratings.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the difference between the coping-continuum and biological-explanation willingness ratings.
choiceExplanations.C: This is correct. The difference is 1.2 points.
choiceExplanations.D: This is larger than the observed difference and closer to a comparison involving stigma ratings.

questions.commonMistake: Subtracting the wrong message conditions or using the stigma column instead of the willingness column.
